headlines.title: Man injured in stabbing in San Jose; police search for suspect
headlines.description: San Jose police on Tuesday were searching for a suspect wanted in a Monday night stabbing that sent a man to the hospital. Police said officers responded at about 10:49 p.m. to a reported stabbing along the 2400 block of La Ragione Avenue, not far from the intersection of Tully and Senter roads. The victim was stabbed at least once and taken to a local hospital for treatment, according to police. His condition wasn’t immediately known. The suspect had not been apprehended as of Tuesday afternoon. An investigation is ongoing. Additional information wasn’t immediately available.

headlines.title: Election officials remind voters of their rights
headlines.description: Some tips for Election Day: Make a plan to vote and don’t walk away while in line to vote when the polls close. With California’s June 2 statewide primary election just a week away, election officials are reminding voters to know their rights. Polls will be open from 7 a.m. to 8 p.m. on June 2. Voters who are still in line when the polls close have the right to vote, so they shouldn’t leave just because it’s 8 p.m., California Secretary of State Shirley Weber said. In addition, registered voters may cast a ballot if even if their name is not on the list at their polling place. They will be given a provisional ballot, and their vote will be counted if elections officials determine they are eligible to vote, Weber said. Voters may get a new ballot if they have made a mistake and haven’t already cast their ballot. Residents voting by mail can drop off their completed vote-by-mail ballot at any polling location or vote center in the state. Voters should sign the ballot return envelope — county elections offices will compare that signature with the signature in the voter’s file before counting the ballot. The California Voter Bill of Rights, printed in the Voter Information Guide sent to every active registered voting household in the state, will also be posted in every polling location and vote center. More information is available at voterguide.sos.ca.gov.

headlines.title: South Bay gas station sells Powerball ticket worth nearly $500,000
headlines.description: A Powerball player in the South Bay scored nearly $500,000 in winnings in Monday night’s draw, according to the California Lottery. A ticket matching the first five numbers in the Powerball draw was sold at the Shell gas station located at 776 North Mathilda Ave. in Sunnyvale, according to the lottery. The exact prize amount was $481,655. The winning numbers in Monday night’s draw were 17-32-48-60-64 and the Powerball 10. No jackpot tickets were sold in the multi-state Powerball draw, so the jackpot rises to an estimated $154 million for the next draw Wednesday, the lottery said.

headlines.title: 4 injured, dozens displaced following fire at Redwood City apartment complex
headlines.description: Dozens of people were displaced and several injured Monday evening when a fire ripped through a Redwood City apartment building. The fire was first reported at 5:18 p.m. at a three-story, 10-unit apartment complex in the 1100 block of Whipple Avenue. The three-alarm fire took an hour to knock down, according to Redwood City Fire Department Deputy Chief Shon Buford. Twenty-three people were displaced and at least four people were transported to the hospital with injuries, he said. Some pets in the building escaped the fire but several were found deceased or are missing. Details as to the cause of the fire are still under investigation at this time, according to Buford.

headlines.title: 10 years later, investigators search for leads in kidnapping of Pearl Pinson
headlines.description: Investigators are searching for new leads that will help them find a Vallejo girl who was kidnapped on her way to school 10 years ago. Pearl Pinson, 15, was kidnapped on May 25, 2016, in Vallejo and has never been seen since. A witness reported seeing a male dragging Pinson, who was bleeding and screaming for help, on an Interstate Highway 780 over-crossing in Vallejo around 7 a.m. The suspect was identified as Fernando Castro, 19, and was found driving south on U.S. Highway 101 in San Luis Obispo County in a gold Saturn. Local officers tried to pull him over, but he fled and officers pursued until he eventually stopped exited in Santa Barbara County. He began shooting at law enforcement officers and was shot and killed when officers returned fire. “Unfortunately, he may have taken the secret of what happened to Pearl with him to his grave,” the Solano County Sheriff’s Office said in a news release on Monday. Information from Castro’s cellphone and a search of the Saturn led investigators to look for Pearl in the Jenner area of Sonoma County but she was not located. Search and rescue crews also scoured the Glen Cove area in Vallejo, an open field and stream near the abduction site at the I-780 pedestrian over-crossing in Vallejo and state Highway 37 between Vallejo and Sonoma County, including Skaggs Island. Anyone who has information that help find Pearl is asked to contact the Solano County Sheriff’s Office tip line at ( 707)784-1963. All tips can remain anonymous.

headlines.title: New insurance deal aims to solve ‘crisis,' offer double-digit discounts to you
headlines.description: Insuring your home isn’t as easy or as cheap as it used to be. As we’ve been reporting the past few years, you’re paying more than ever. And families have told us they fear losing their home policy with little notice. But now, two companies say they are partnering to shake up homeowners’ insurance coast to coast. To understand the situation, we headed to wine country. “This is a dream home for my wife and I,” said Healdsburg homeowner Greg Dexter. At the Dexter home, you might expect to see sprinklers on the sprawling vineyard and in the flowery garden. But Dexter points up. “You can see the copper pipe here and the little emitters,” he said. Dexter has sprinklers curiously installed on his eaves and roof. “It’s like a giant car wash,” he said, describing his home’s self-defense rig to shield against wildfire. His family paid dearly to install the system after his insurance company dropped him because his home was too risky. “It’s not gonna stop the vineyard from burning, but it’s gonna sprinkle the whole house,” Dexter said. “It’ll rain around the whole house and the embers will go out.” The Dexters have Frontline, a system that automatically sprays down a house with a light mist when a wildfire burns close by. “This takes it down to about 10% chance of burning when the sprinkler system’s operating,” Dexter said. Last year, the Los Angeles fires put Frontline sprinklers to the test. We saw firsthand. In Palisades, a Frontline customer’s home was still standing. But all their neighbors’ homes had burned down. We asked homeowner and Frontline customer Vardaan Vasisht what his home would look like today if he did not have the sprinklers. “I’m pretty sure it wouldn’t be there,” he said. Frontline was founded by ecologist Harry Statter. “We created almost like a water bubble,” he said in April 2025. Back then, Statter told NBC Bay Area he had 61 customer homes in the LA fire zones – 59 of those homes survived. He said insurance companies took note. “We’ve had a number of insurance companies contact us,” Statter said. “They want to develop programs.” That’s now happening. Frontline says it is now partnering with Stand insurance, a San Francisco startup we first showed you in 2024. “We’re a young company,” said CEO Dan Preston. He says Stand’s team – including aerospace experts, physics, and artificial intelligence engineers – saw how well the Frontline nozzles performed. Stand then developed a new computer model. And now, Stand will offer Frontline homeowners a discount up to 25%. “Frontline customers can get a discount because we know that models out to have a less risky home,” Preston said. Not everyone can afford something as elaborate as a sprinkler system like Frontline, costing tens of thousands of dollars, depending on how big your home is. But there’s something for the rest of us in this new deal. Preston told me their new modeling, called the Stand World Model, can help other homeowners, like you, get cheaper coverage when you make less expensive updates to your home. It starts with a 3D view of your home’s risks. Stand can then manipulate that with your future efforts to mitigate those risks. “We can basically pinpoint,” Preston said. “These trees, if you remove them, your premium comes down by $5,000. Or if you change to a different type of siding, it’ll come down by $6,000.” Besides the Stand World Model helping with California’s insurance crisis, Preston said Stand is looking at Florida’s equally frustrating insurance market. Big name insurance companies say they are starting to use catastrophe models, too. The American Property Casualty Insurance Association told us, “Insurers are rewarding wildfire mitigation, but the biggest savings come when homeowners take a comprehensive, science-based approach—not just one-off fixes.” Back in wine country, Dexter says his family’s personal insurance crisis is over. “Crisis is a strong word, but it really is,” he said. “For many, many people, their home is their most valuable asset.” The Dexters have assurance with their new sprinklers, plus insurance, finally. Next, Dexter would love to see other carriers resume selling policies and bring back competition. He believes his home is a good bet for an insurance company. A model home, of sorts. “Oh yeah,” he said. california wildfires May 21 california wildfires Sep 9, 2025 california wildfires Sep 3, 2025 Wildfire devastates historic California gold mining town, homes burn in blaze

headlines.title: Miami Dolphins legend and Oakland native Manny Fernandez dies at 79
headlines.description: Former Miami Dolphins defensive lineman and Oakland native Manny Fernandez has died at the age of 79, the team announced on social media. Fernandez was a key member of the Dolphins’ famed “No-Name Defense,” which led Miami to the only undefeated and untied season in NFL history. The Dolphins finished 17-0 during the 1972 season and went on to win the Super Bowl. Fernandez was born in Oakland and attended San Lorenzo High School. He played on all three Dolphins Super Bowl teams in the early 1970s and helped Miami win championships in 1972 and 1973.

headlines.title: Mental health report delayed in Molotov cocktail case at Sam Altman's SF home
headlines.description: A mental health report expected to be released to attorneys Tuesday in the case of a Texas man accused of throwing a Molotov cocktail at the San Francisco home of OpenAI CEO Sam Altman was delayed. Defense attorneys allege 20-year-old Daniel Moreno-Gama was experiencing a mental health crisis at the time prosecutors say he threw the Molotov cocktail. The report was one of the initial requests made by the public defender during Moreno-Gama’s arraignment a couple of weeks ago. Prosecutors say Moreno-Gama threw a Molotov cocktail at the gate of Altman’s Russian Hill home during the early morning hours of April 10 with intent to harm. The fire caused damage, but no one was injured. Prosecutors say Moreno-Gama then continued to OpenAI headquarters, where he allegedly attempted to damage property there. State prosecutors have charged Moreno-Gama with nearly a dozen counts, including attempted murder, arson and possession of a destructive device. He is also facing federal charges. Moreno-Gama’s preliminary hearing was originally scheduled for mid-May but has since been delayed. Attorneys now are expected to receive and review the mental health report July 8. San Francisco May 15 Artificial intelligence Apr 13 San Francisco Apr 10 Suspect in Molotov cocktail attack on Sam Altman's San Francisco home identified

headlines.title: Los Gatos mother convicted of throwing underage parties faces sentencing
headlines.description: A Los Gatos mother convicted of holding alcohol-fueled sexualized parties for teens is scheduled to be sentenced this week in Santa Clara County Superior Court. Shannon O’Connor, also know as Shannon Bruga, faces more than 30 years in prison after jurors in March convicted her on 48 felony charges. Judge Elizabeth Peterson previously increased the potential maximum prison sentencing, saying O’Connor was sophisticated in orchestrating the underage parties and sexual assaults. O’Connor may receive less than that, but it won’t be clear until the judge announces the sentencing, which she has wide discretion over. Prosecutors said O’Connor provided alcohol and condoms for parties attended by teenagers as young as 14 and 15 years old and encouraged sex acts during the gatherings. The sentencing hearing started on Tuesday and is expected to continue until Thursday. About 15 victims and their parents are scheduled to give victim impact statements before the judge announces O’Connor’s sentencing. Los Gatos Mar 4 Los Gatos mom Mar 4 During the first day of the sentencing hearing Tuesday, Jane Doe 4 spoke to O’Connor in court. “I am now 20 years old, and I can clearly understand how disgusting your actions were toward children. When you were involved in my life, I was 14, still just a kid, I didn’t fully understand how young I was, but now I do,” Jane Doe 4 said. In March, a jury convicted O’Connor of 16 felonies that include 13 child endangerment counts, two sexual penetration counts and one count of dissuading a witness. Prosecutors say the sexual misconduct counts involve O’Connor orchestrating non-consensual sexual activity between some of the teens, for instance pushing a boy into a room with an intoxicated girl, investigators said. O’Connor denied that allegation in court through her attorney. Prosecutors say O’Connor will have to register as a sexual predator as a result of her convictions. O’Connor has repeatedly denied the sexual assault allegations against her. “It’s so unfair. A lot of these stories are just not true. And I’ve been affected deeply by these stories in jail. And I have been beaten up multiple times. They call me a pedophile. They think I’m this rapist. They think that I’m a horrible monster. That I didn’t do,” O’Connor told NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit. “I am sorry for what everyone has had to go through. I never intended for any harm to happen to these kids.”

headlines.title: Video shows Alcatraz ferry hitting San Francisco pier
headlines.description: Dozens of passengers were onboard a cruise Sunday in San Francisco when the boat scraped along the Pier 31 dock and began breaking out windows. The damage was caught on cell phone video. NBC Bay Area reached out to Alcatraz City Cruises, which said there were no reported injuries. The company said an investigation is underway to determine the cause of the damage. A representative from the Port of San Francisco said Alcatraz City Cruises leases the dock space from the city and would be responsible for any damages. Gia Vang spoke to one of the passengers. Watch her report in the video above.

headlines.title: New questions surround Fairfield police officer's actions seen in viral videos
headlines.description: New videos have surfaced involving an officer seen in previous viral footage taking down a student in Fairfield. The fallout comes after students and parents rallied last week over what they say is excessive use of force. Video that went viral last week shows a police officer hitting a student in the face multiple times while police responded to a fight at Fairfield High School. Another video that recently surfaced shows the same officer pulling an 18-year-old girl out of a vehicle by the hair during a traffic stop last July. “It’s ridiculous that we have an officer with a badge that operates in this kind of way and this kind of manner,” said Berry Accius, a community advocate. Fairfield May 22 Fairfield May 21 The police officer used similar tactics on the high school student last week by pulling the 16-year-old student’s hair. “She shouldn’t be on the force,” said Andrew Holiman, a rapper in Fairfield. Holiman was outraged when he saw what happened to the Fairfield High School teen and he immediately recognized the officer. “Watching the video I noticed it was the officer I had an encounter with myself,” he said. Holiman said he and his friends were shooting a music video in his apartment complex parking lot when the same officer along with nine others came rushing in accusing them of breaking cars. He said the officer approached them very aggressively, making them sit on the curb as police searched their car. When Holiman asked for the officer’s badge number she refused. Police determined Holiman and his friends weren’t breaking the law and ended up releasing the men. But when Holiman saw what happened to the student he knew he had to speak out. “I just felt like being a part of the community is to show that this is a pattern for this officer — she’s violent,” he said. “She’s reckless.” NBC Bay Area reached out to the Fairfield Police Department for comment on the videos that have surfaced since last week’s incident at Fairfield High School. The police chief said the officer involved has been administratively reassigned and an outside investigation will take place. Meanwhile, another protest about the officer’s actions is scheduled Tuesday at Fairfield City Hall.

headlines.title: Considering a summer barbecue? Grilling will cost you more this year
headlines.description: As Memorial Day weekend signals the unofficial start of summer, be prepared to pay more if you’re planning to host a backyard barbecue. Beef prices are up 16% from this time last year, hot dogs are up 11%, seasonings and spices are up 4%, and tomatoes are up 40%. “As we all know, everything’s smaller and everything’s more expensive,” Ricardo Ballin of San Jose said. “The bags of charcoal, you used to be able to struggle carrying them. Now you pick up two bags and I think it was like $15.” NBC Bay Area’s Scott Budman has more in the video report above.
